summary: Reporter observes missing closing </svg> tag when using SKSvgCanvas with SKManagedWStream wrapping a MemoryStream. The root cause is that MemoryStream.ToArray() is called before the SVG canvas is disposed — the closing tag is written by the native SkSVGDevice destructor during canvas disposal.

stepsToReproduce: Create a MemoryStream, wrap it in SKManagedWStream, create SKSvgCanvas
Call MemoryStream.ToArray() while canvas is still alive (before Dispose)
Observe the SVG output is missing the closing </svg> tag

screenshots.description: SVG source showing missing closing tag
SVG renders correctly when closing tag is manually added

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The SVG closing tag has always been written by the native SkSVGDevice destructor during canvas disposal. The reporter's code reads the stream before disposal in both 2.x and 3.x. The claimed regression is likely due to a code restructuring or unrelated change.

regression.reason: The closing </svg> tag is written by the SkSVGDevice destructor (SkSVGDevice.cpp:750-764). This has been the behavior since the SVG canvas was introduced. The reporter's code reads the stream before disposal, which would produce incomplete SVG in any version.

analysis.summary: The SVG closing tag is written by the native SkSVGDevice destructor when the canvas is disposed. The reporter's code uses 'using var' declarations (C# 8 using declarations) which defer disposal to the end of the enclosing scope, then reads MemoryStream.ToArray() before that scope ends. This means the canvas hasn't been disposed (and the closing tag hasn't been written) when the data is read. The SkiaSharp test suite demonstrates the correct pattern: read the stream after the canvas's using block ends.
analysis.rationale: Classified as by-design because the SVG closing tag has always been written during canvas disposal — this is standard behavior for Skia's SkSVGCanvas. The reporter's code reads the stream before disposing the canvas. The reporter's observation that SKFileWStream works is consistent: they likely read the file after the using block ends. Issue #2204 documents the same root cause. While the reporter claims regression from 2.88.9, the native behavior hasn't changed.

keySignals.text: result.Data = managedStream.ToArray() is called inside the same scope as using var canvas
keySignals.interpretation: MemoryStream data is read before canvas disposal — the closing </svg> tag hasn't been written yet.

keySignals.text: Looking into it, it might be SKManagedWStream specific. SKFileWStream seems to output a correct SVG with close tags.
keySignals.interpretation: With SKFileWStream, the file is likely read after disposal completes, so the closing tag is present. The difference is when the data is read, not the stream type.

keySignals.text: The root <svg> tag gets closed by the destructor.
keySignals.interpretation: This is Skia's documented design — the closing tag is written when the device (and thus canvas) is destroyed.

codeInvestigation.finding: Comment on line 750 states 'The root <svg> tag gets closed by the destructor.' The fRootElement (AutoElement for 'svg') is a unique_ptr that writes the closing tag when destroyed in ~SkSVGDevice().

SKSvgCanvas.Create(SKRect, SKWStream) calls sk_svgcanvas_create_with_stream and returns the canvas via SKObject.Referenced. The canvas owns the native SVG device, so disposing the canvas triggers the destructor that writes </svg>.
Test SvgCanvasSavesFile shows the correct pattern: canvas is created in a using block, stream data is read AFTER the block ends (after canvas disposal). This confirms the expected usage.

OnWrite delegates to the managed stream's Write method. No buffering or delayed write — data written by native code is immediately available in the managed stream. The stream itself is not the problem.

workarounds: Dispose the SKSvgCanvas (and SKManagedWStream) before reading MemoryStream.ToArray() by using scoped using blocks instead of using var declarations
nextQuestions: Did the reporter's code structure change between 2.88.9 and 3.x that affected disposal ordering?

resolution.hypothesis: The reporter reads MemoryStream.ToArray() before the SVG canvas is disposed. The closing </svg> tag is written during canvas disposal by the native SkSVGDevice destructor. Using 'using var' defers disposal to end of scope, but the read happens before scope end.

proposals.title: Use scoped using blocks for proper disposal ordering
proposals.description: Replace 'using var' declarations with scoped 'using' blocks so the canvas and stream are disposed before reading the MemoryStream. This ensures the closing </svg> tag is written before ToArray() is called.

proposals.title: Use the Stream overload directly
proposals.description: Use SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, memoryStream) with the .NET Stream directly, avoiding manual SKManagedWStream creation. The canvas internally manages the wrapper stream. Still requires disposal before reading.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Use scoped using blocks for proper disposal ordering
resolution.recommendedReason: Minimal change to the reporter's code — just restructure the using statements to ensure the canvas is disposed before reading the stream. This matches the pattern used in SkiaSharp's own test suite.

actionability.reason: The closing </svg> tag is written during canvas disposal by design (Skia's SkSVGDevice destructor). The reporter reads the stream before disposal. This is a usage pattern issue, not a bug in SkiaSharp. The correct pattern is demonstrated in the test suite and in issue #2204.

actions.description: Apply bug, SkiaSharp, SVG backend labels
Explain by-design behavior and provide workaround with correct disposal ordering
Link to #2204 which documents the same disposal ordering issue with SKSvgCanvas
Close as by-design — the behavior is correct, reporter needs to dispose canvas before reading stream
